It is the predicate of 'wujooh' in the nominative, describing the faces as radiant.

80:38.irab_en: This is a nominal sentence. 'Wujoohun' is the subject (mubtada') in the nominative; its indefiniteness is acceptable here because it carries a partitive sense ('some faces') and because the adverb specifying it follows. 'Yawma'idhin' is an adverb of time in the accusative, linked to the predicate, locating the description on the Day of Resurrection. 'Musfiratun' is the predicate (khabar) in the nominative, agreeing with the subject and describing those faces as bright and beaming. The sentence thus introduces one of two contrasting groups on that Day, this group marked by light and joy, the other (in verse 40) by dust and gloom.

faqs_en.q: How can 'wujoohun' be a subject when it is indefinite?
faqs_en.a: An indefinite subject is permitted when it gains specification, here through the partitive sense ('some faces') and the following descriptive context, so it validly serves as the mubtada'.

faqs_en.q: What is the predicate of this verse?
faqs_en.a: 'Musfiratun' is the predicate, in the nominative, describing the faces as bright and shining.

faqs_en.q: Why is 'yawma'idhin' accusative?
faqs_en.a: It is an adverb of time, which takes the accusative, and it is connected to the meaning of the predicate.

80:39.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Abasa Ayah 39 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
80:39.tldr_en: This verse continues describing the bright faces as laughing and rejoicing. Both 'laughing' and 'rejoicing at good news' are further predicates or attributes of the same 'faces' from the previous verse, all in the nominative.

words_en.note_en: It is an additional predicate of 'wujooh' from the previous verse, in the nominative, describing the faces as laughing.
It is a further predicate of the same subject in the nominative, describing the faces as delighted with good tidings.

80:39.irab_en: Both words in this verse continue the description of 'wujooh' (faces) introduced in verse 38. 'Dahikatun' is an additional predicate (khabar) of that subject, in the nominative and agreeing with it, portraying the faces as laughing. 'Mustabshiratun' is yet another predicate, also nominative, depicting them as rejoicing at glad tidings. Arabic readily allows multiple predicates for a single subject, so 'wujooh' is here described in succession as bright (musfirah), laughing (dahikah), and joyful (mustabshirah). Alternatively, these may be read as successive adjectives qualifying the faces; either analysis keeps them all in the nominative in agreement with 'wujooh.'

faqs_en.q: Can a single subject have more than one predicate?
faqs_en.a: Yes. Arabic allows a subject to take several predicates in sequence, so 'wujooh' is described as shining, then laughing, then rejoicing, each in the nominative.

faqs_en.q: Why are both words nominative?
faqs_en.a: They agree with their subject 'wujooh,' which is nominative; whether taken as predicates or as adjectives, they follow it in case.

faqs_en.q: What is the subject these words describe?
faqs_en.a: They describe 'wujoohun' (faces) mentioned in the preceding verse 38, continuing its description.

80:40.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Abasa Ayah 40 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
80:40.tldr_en: This verse describes another set of faces on that Day having dust upon them. 'Faces' is a subject, and the prepositional phrase 'upon them' functions as a fronted predicate before the delayed subject 'dust.'
80:40.translation_en: And other faces, that Day, will have dust upon them.

words_en.note_en: Preceded by the connective 'wa,' it is a subject in the nominative introducing the second, contrasting group of faces.
An adverb of time in the accusative, linked to the predicate of the sentence.
The prepositional phrase 'alay-ha' is placed first to serve as the predicate of the delayed subject.
It is the delayed subject in the nominative, since the prepositional predicate preceded it.

80:40.irab_en: The connective 'wa' joins this sentence to the previous description. 'Wujoohun' is the subject (mubtada') in the nominative, introducing a second, contrasting group. 'Yawma'idhin' is an adverb of time in the accusative, tied to the sentence's predicate. The phrase 'alay-ha' (a preposition plus attached pronoun, in the genitive) is fronted to act as the predicate; consequently 'ghabaratun' (dust) comes after it as the delayed subject in the nominative. So the structure is a nominal sentence within the larger one: 'alay-ha ghabaratun' ('upon them is dust') itself serves as the predicate describing 'wujooh,' contrasting these dust-covered faces with the radiant ones before.

faqs_en.q: Why is 'ghabaratun' the delayed subject?
faqs_en.a: Because its predicate, the prepositional phrase 'alay-ha,' was placed before it. When the predicate is fronted, the noun that follows becomes the delayed subject and stays nominative.

faqs_en.q: What does the inner sentence 'alay-ha ghabaratun' do?
faqs_en.a: It is itself a nominal sentence ('upon them is dust') that serves as the predicate describing the main subject 'wujooh.'

faqs_en.q: What is the function of 'wa' at the start?
faqs_en.a: It is a connective joining this verse to the preceding description, introducing the contrasting second group of faces.

80:41.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Abasa Ayah 41 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
80:41.tldr_en: This verse describes the dust-covered faces as overcast by gloom. The verb 'covers them' takes the attached pronoun as its object, and 'darkness/gloom' is its subject, the whole clause describing the faces of the previous verse.

words_en.note_en: An imperfect verb in the nominative; its attached pronoun 'ha' is the direct object, and the clause describes the faces.
It is the doer of the verb 'tarhaqu,' in the nominative, meaning a gloom or blackness overspreads them.

80:41.irab_en: This verse forms a verbal sentence that continues describing the faces of verse 40. 'Tarhaquha' is an imperfect verb in the nominative (nothing makes it accusative or jussive), and the attached pronoun 'ha' is its direct object, referring back to those faces. 'Qataratun' is the subject (fa'il) of the verb, in the nominative, meaning a dark gloom or blackness. The whole verbal sentence functions as a further descriptive predicate of 'wujooh,' so the picture is completed: those faces already have dust upon them, and now a gloomy blackness covers them as well, the opposite of the shining, laughing faces described earlier.

faqs_en.q: What is the subject of 'tarhaqu'?
faqs_en.a: 'Qataratun' (gloom/blackness) is the subject, in the nominative, while the attached pronoun 'ha' is the object referring to the faces.

faqs_en.q: Why is the verb feminine ('tarhaqu')?
faqs_en.a: The verb agrees with its feminine subject 'qataratun,' so it takes the feminine prefix even though it precedes the subject.

faqs_en.q: What does this clause describe?
faqs_en.a: The whole verbal sentence serves as a further description of the dust-covered faces from the preceding verse, depicting gloom overspreading them.

80:42.h1_en: I'rab of Surah Abasa Ayah 42 — word-by-word Arabic grammar
80:42.tldr_en: This concluding verse identifies the owners of those gloomy faces as the disbelievers, the wicked. 'Those' is the subject, 'they' is a separating pronoun, and 'the disbelievers' is the predicate, followed by 'the wicked' as its attribute.
80:42.translation_en: Those are the disbelievers, the wicked.

words_en.note_en: A demonstrative pronoun, indeclinable, in the nominative position as the subject of the sentence.
It is a pronoun of separation with no case role, emphasizing and distinguishing the subject from the predicate.
It is the predicate of 'ula'ika' in the nominative, identifying those faces' owners as the disbelievers.
It is an adjective describing 'al-kafarah' in the nominative, characterizing them further as the wicked, immoral ones.

80:42.irab_en: This is a nominal sentence closing the sura's contrast. 'Ula'ika' is a demonstrative pronoun, indeclinable but occupying the nominative position as the subject (mubtada'). 'Humu' is a separating pronoun (damir fasl) carrying no independent case role; it adds emphasis and marks off the subject from the predicate, also stressing exclusivity. 'Al-kafaratu' is the predicate (khabar) in the nominative, identifying those people as the disbelievers. 'Al-fajaratu' follows as an attribute (na't) of 'al-kafarah,' also nominative, further describing them as the wicked and corrupt. The verse thus names the owners of the dust-covered, gloomy faces, sealing the contrast with the radiant believers.

faqs_en.q: What is the role of 'humu' in this verse?
faqs_en.a: It is a separating pronoun (damir fasl) with no case ending of its own; it emphasizes the sentence and distinguishes the subject from the predicate, conveying exclusivity.

faqs_en.q: Why is 'al-fajaratu' nominative?
faqs_en.a: It is an attribute (na't) describing 'al-kafarah,' so it follows that word in case, which is nominative as the predicate.

faqs_en.q: Is 'ula'ika' declinable?
faqs_en.a: No. As a demonstrative pronoun it is indeclinable, but it occupies the nominative position functioning as the subject of the sentence.
